_work.title: Nicolas Flamel & the Hieroglyphic Figures
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The image-corpus of the most famous alchemical legend: the Parisian scrivener Nicolas Flamel (d. 1418) who, with his wife Pernelle, was said to have decoded the Book of Abraham the Jew and achieved the Philosophers' Stone, then painted his cryptic 'hieroglyphic figures' on an arch of the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents. The set gathers the engraved figures (dragons, the resurrection, the King and the slaughtered innocents, Saints Peter and Paul) as reproduced in the 1612 *Livre des figures hiéroglyphiques*, the figures of the Book of Abraham, portraits of Flamel and Pernelle, his tombstone and charnel-house epitaph, his surviving house on the rue de Montmorency, and the Tour Saint-Jacques that bears his monogram.

entries.title: The Two Dragons (Green & Red), *Livre des figures hiéroglyphiques*
entries.creator: after Nicolas Flamel (engraving)
entries.summary:
The two fighting dragons — one winged and red, one wingless and dark — the most reproduced of the hieroglyphic figures Nicolas Flamel was said to have painted at the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents, here in the engraved version printed in the *Livre des figures hiéroglyphiques* (1612).

## Iconography
Two serpentine dragons coil and bite at one another in a barren landscape: one bears wings, the other none. Flamel's own commentary (as printed) glosses them as the two principles of the work — the volatile and the fixed — locked in the struggle from which the philosophical matter is born.

## Significance
The [[motif:green and red dragons|two dragons]] became shorthand for the alchemical marriage of opposites: [[motif:sulphur and mercury|sulphur and mercury]], hot and cold, the wingless 'fixed' body and the winged 'volatile' spirit. Transmitted through the 1612 edition attributed to Arnauld de la Chevalerie and the 1624 English *Exposition* of Eirenaeus Orandus, the figure anchored the Flamel legend at the centre of early-modern alchemical iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A clean dual-boss silhouette: one creature winged and fast, one earthbound and heavy, that must be made to fight each other.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: The Procession (Figure I of the Hieroglyphic Figures)
entries.creator: Denys Molinier (engraving after Flamel)
entries.summary:
The opening procession of Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures, engraved by Denys Molinier among the plates of the *Livre des figures hiéroglyphiques* that reproduce the paintings Flamel claimed to set on the fourth arch of the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents.

## Iconography
Robed figures move in solemn file across the panel, the first of the painted scenes Flamel said he placed at the charnel arch. In his printed exposition each grouping is read as a station of the Great Work rather than a literal funeral.

## Significance
Flamel framed the whole charnel-arch programme as a [[motif:hieroglyphic figures|hieroglyphic]] sermon: outwardly a Christian memento mori for the dead of the [[motif:innocents cemetery|Innocents]], inwardly a coded sequence of the alchemical operations. The plates engraved by Molinier are the form in which most readers met these now-lost paintings, fixing the iconography of the legend for centuries.

## For artists & game designers
A processional intro cutscene whose figures each foreshadow a later stage of the player's transformation.

entries.title: The Resurrection of the Dead (Figure of the Hieroglyphic Figures)
entries.creator: Denys Molinier (engraving after Flamel)
entries.summary:
The Resurrection of the Dead from Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures, engraved by Denys Molinier — naked bodies rising from their graves, read by Flamel as a figure of the final stage of the alchemical work.

## Iconography
The dead climb from the earth at the call of a trumpet, an image drawn from the Christian Last Judgment. In Flamel's exposition the risen bodies stand for the matter brought back to life, perfected and multiplied after passing through dissolution.

## Significance
The [[motif:resurrection of the dead|resurrection]] is the governing emblem of the charnel-arch programme: placed fittingly at a cemetery, it lets Flamel align the Christian hope of bodily rising with the alchemical [[motif:multiplication|multiplication]] and reddening of the Stone. The doubling of sacred and chemical meaning is exactly the 'hieroglyphic' method his book advertises.

## For artists & game designers
A revival mechanic visualised as the dead rising in ranks — a literal respawn shrine that also signals the final upgrade tier.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: The King & the Slaughter of the Innocents (Hieroglyphic Figures)
entries.creator: Denys Molinier (engraving after Flamel)
entries.summary:
The crowned King ordering the slaughter of the innocents, from Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures engraved by Denys Molinier — the most notorious and bloodiest of the charnel-arch scenes.

## Iconography
A king on his throne commands armed soldiers who put infants to the sword; the children's blood is gathered up. The scene quotes the biblical Massacre of the Innocents under Herod, doubly apt for paintings set at the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents.

## Significance
Flamel's exposition reads the [[motif:massacre of the innocents|spilt blood of the children]] as the [[motif:philosophical mercury|philosophical mercury]] required for the reddening — a deliberately shocking veil over a chemical operation. The image became a stock example of how violently literal alchemical 'decknamen' (cover-names) could be, and of the legend's taste for sacred-profane double meaning.

## For artists & game designers
A morally charged set-piece: an atrocity in the surface story that, decoded, is only a recipe — a puzzle about reading past the literal.

citations.text: Lawrence M. Principe, *The Secrets of Alchemy* (2013).

entries.title: The Dragons of the Hieroglyphic Figures (engraved by Molinier)
entries.creator: Denys Molinier (engraving after Flamel)
entries.summary:
The fighting dragons of Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures in Denys Molinier's eighteenth-century engraved version, a companion to the famous title-cut of the two serpents.

## Iconography
Two dragons, one winged and one without wings, twist together in mortal combat at the foot of the charnel arch. Flamel's text names them as the two natures whose battle and union generate the philosophical matter.

## Significance
Within the engraved cycle the [[motif:green and red dragons|dragons]] state the central theme — the strife and reconciliation of the [[motif:fixed and volatile|fixed and the volatile]] — that the rest of the figures elaborate. Molinier's plates standardised the look of these creatures for generations of readers who never saw the lost originals.

## For artists & game designers
A recurring sigil motif: two intertwined serpents that the player meets first as enemies and later as a unified emblem of mastery.

citations.text: Stanislas Klossowski de Rola, *The Golden Game* (1988).

entries.title: Saints Peter & Paul (Figure of the Hieroglyphic Figures)
entries.creator: Denys Molinier (engraving after Flamel)
entries.summary:
Saints Peter and Paul from Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures, engraved by Denys Molinier — a devotional pair that Flamel's exposition reads as the twin principles of the alchemical work.

## Iconography
The two apostles stand as a balanced pair, Peter with his keys and Paul with his sword, framed in the manner of an altar image. In Flamel's text the saints to whom he professed devotion double as emblems of the two complementary matters that must be joined.

## Significance
Placing the patrons [[motif:saint peter|Peter]] and [[motif:saint paul|Paul]] at the head of the figures let Flamel cloak the work in unimpeachable piety while quietly assigning them the roles of [[motif:sol and luna|sun and moon]], fixed and volatile. It is a model instance of the legend's strategy of hiding chemistry inside orthodox Christian imagery.

## For artists & game designers
A paired-patron UI: two saints whose attributes (key vs. sword) cue two opposed but complementary play styles.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: Figure I from the *Book of Abraham the Jew*
entries.creator: after the legendary *Book of Abraham the Jew*
entries.date: engraving after a legend of c.1400
entries.summary:
A figure reproduced from the legendary *Book of Abraham the Jew* — the gilt-leaved volume Nicolas Flamel claimed to have bought and, after years of study, to have decoded, achieving the Philosophers' Stone.

## Iconography
The plate shows one of the book's enigmatic images — serpents and a rod recalling the [[motif:caduceus|caduceus]] of Mercury, the kind of figure Flamel said filled the manuscript between its pages of crabbed writing. The originals were always described, never securely seen, so the engravings reconstruct the legend's lost source.

## Significance
The [[motif:book of abraham the jew|Book of Abraham the Jew]] is the mythic origin of the whole Flamel story: a Kabbalistic-alchemical codex by a fictitious Jewish sage, intelligible only after pilgrimage and inspired help. As a 'book that cannot be found,' it became one of esotericism's most enduring images of forbidden, half-lost knowledge.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate MacGuffin tome: a fragmentary picture-book whose plates are themselves the quest, decoded scene by scene.

citations.text: Lawrence M. Principe, *The Secrets of Alchemy* (2013).

entries.title: Figure III from the *Book of Abraham the Jew*
entries.creator: after the legendary *Book of Abraham the Jew*
entries.date: engraving after a legend of c.1400
entries.summary:
A further figure from the legendary *Book of Abraham the Jew*, the cryptic manuscript at the root of the Flamel legend, reproduced in engraved form.

## Iconography
The plate carries another of the book's allegorical scenes — serpents and emblematic bodies — meant to encode a stage of the work without words. Flamel's narrative dwells on his long inability to read such pictures until a learned Jew at Santiago de Compostela helped unlock them.

## Significance
The figures of [[motif:book of abraham the jew|Abraham the Jew]] dramatise the alchemical conviction that the deepest secrets are entrusted to images rather than plain text. The motif of a hidden Hebrew wisdom decoded only through pilgrimage links the Flamel story to the wider Christian fascination with [[motif:lost manuscript|Kabbalah]] and lost ancient learning.

## For artists & game designers
A second codex page that only makes sense once an NPC sage is found and consulted — gating the puzzle behind a journey.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: Composite Plate of Flamel's Hieroglyphic Figures
entries.creator: after Nicolas Flamel (composite engraving)
entries.date: early modern engraving (after a legend of c.1400)
entries.summary:
A composite plate gathering Nicolas Flamel's hieroglyphic figures together, the way the painted arch of the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents was reconstructed for print.

## Iconography
The sheet assembles the principal scenes — the processions, the rising dead, the dragons and the saintly figures — in the arrangement Flamel said he gave the fourth arch of the charnel ground, so the whole programme can be read at a glance.

## Significance
The composite makes plain that the [[motif:hieroglyphic figures|hieroglyphic figures]] were conceived as a single, legible programme: a public monument that was simultaneously a pious memorial and an encrypted treatise. It is the form in which Flamel's lost arch survives in cultural memory, the painted original having vanished with the demolition of the cemetery.

## For artists & game designers
A single 'level map' panel: one image whose tiled scenes are the stations the player must pass through in order.

entries.title: Nicolas Flamel — Line Engraving (Wellcome Collection)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A line-engraved portrait of Nicolas Flamel, the Parisian scrivener (libraire-juré) whose name became synonymous with successful alchemy, from the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
Flamel is shown bust- or half-length in the sober gown of a fourteenth-century Parisian bookseller, presented as a grave and pious man rather than a flamboyant magus. Such retrospective portraits are imaginative reconstructions, the historical Flamel having left no contemporary likeness.

## Significance
The real Flamel (d. 1418) was a successful scrivener and property-owner whose charitable endowments fed the later legend that he had made gold. From the seventeenth century, printed [[motif:portrait|portraits]] like this turned the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|scrivener]] into the archetypal [[motif:adept|adept]], a figure invoked from the alchemical revival down to modern popular fiction.

## For artists & game designers
A reference sheet for an 'unassuming master' NPC: plain medieval dress, no wizard trappings, all the mystery in the reputation.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: Nicolas Flamel & Pernelle (from Lacroix, *Manners & Customs*)
entries.creator: after Paul Lacroix
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: from Paul Lacroix, *Vie militaire et religieuse au moyen âge*]
entries.summary:
Nicolas Flamel together with his wife Pernelle (Perenelle), reproduced in Paul Lacroix's popular nineteenth-century surveys of medieval life from the carved or painted donor images Flamel set up around Paris.

## Iconography
The couple kneel or stand as paired donors in medieval dress, hands joined in prayer in the manner of a votive monument. Flamel commissioned several such images of himself and Pernelle on church arches and tombs, and they pass here through Lacroix into wide circulation.

## Significance
[[motif:flamel and pernelle|Flamel and Pernelle]] are the archetypal [[motif:alchemical couple|alchemical couple]]: the legend insists the Stone was achieved by husband and wife together, and Pernelle's wealth and shared labour are essential to the story. Their paired donor-image became an emblem of marriage as a working partnership in the Art.

## For artists & game designers
A co-op pair portrait: two married adepts whose abilities only complete the recipe when used in tandem.

citations.text: Paul Lacroix, *Vie militaire et religieuse au moyen âge et à l'époque de la Renaissance* (1873).

entries.title: Tombstone of Nicolas Flamel
entries.creator: Anonymous (Parisian carver)
entries.repository: Musée de Cluny (Musée national du Moyen Âge), Paris
entries.summary:
The carved tombstone of Nicolas Flamel, a genuine surviving relic of the man behind the legend, today held at the Musée de Cluny in Paris.

## Iconography
The stone bears, above an inscription naming Flamel and recording his pious bequests, small carved figures of Christ flanked by Saints Peter and Paul, with the sun and moon and a recumbent body below. It is a conventional late-medieval funerary slab, its restrained imagery a world away from the lurid charnel-arch figures.

## Significance
The [[motif:tombstone|tombstone]] documents the historical Flamel: a prosperous scrivener whose real charity, not transmutation, paid for his monuments. Its survival — long after the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|legend]] outgrew the man — lets historians separate the documented Parisian bourgeois from the immortal adept of folklore.

## For artists & game designers
A real artifact to anchor a tall tale: a plain gravestone the player can examine to learn the unglamorous truth behind a legend.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: Epitaph of Nicolas Flamel (Musée de Cluny)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Parisian carver)
entries.repository: Musée de Cluny (Musée national du Moyen Âge), Paris
entries.summary:
A high-resolution photograph of Nicolas Flamel's carved epitaph stone, preserved at the Musée de Cluny, recording in gothic script his name, death, and charitable foundations.

## Iconography
The slab combines a dense gothic inscription with small relief figures of holy persons and a corpse, in the standard idiom of an early-fifteenth-century Parisian funerary monument. The text asks prayers for Flamel's soul and details the alms and chapels he endowed.

## Significance
The [[motif:epitaph|epitaph]] is primary evidence for the documented Flamel and the genuine wealth that, redistributed as conspicuous [[motif:pious bequest|charity]], seeded the rumour of made gold. Modern scholarship leans on stones like this to reconstruct a bourgeois benefactor quite distinct from the immortal [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|adept]] of legend.

## For artists & game designers
An epigraphy prop: a weathered inscription whose deciphering rewards the player with the real backstory.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: The House of Nicolas Flamel, 51 rue de Montmorency
entries.creator: Georges-Henri Manesse (drawing)
entries.summary:
A drawing of the surviving house Nicolas Flamel built in 1407 at 51 rue de Montmorency, the 'maison du grand pignon' — reputedly the oldest stone house in Paris.

## Iconography
The sheet records the tall gabled facade with its carved frieze of saints, angels, and labourers and its inscription asking each tenant to say a prayer for the dead. Flamel raised it not as a laboratory but as a lodging-house whose poor occupants prayed for his soul.

## Significance
The [[motif:flamel house|house]] is a tangible monument of the historical Flamel's [[motif:almshouse|charity]] — the very generosity later read as proof of alchemical riches. Pilgrims and treasure-hunters long searched it for hidden gold, and it remains a stop on the Parisian map of the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|Flamel legend]].

## For artists & game designers
A real explorable landmark: a carved medieval facade whose inscriptions are clues, ransacked by past seekers for a hoard that was never there.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: Monument of Nicolas Flamel: Effigy, House & Plan
entries.creator: Claude-Louis Bernier (drawing)
entries.summary:
An antiquarian drawing assembling the monuments of Nicolas Flamel — his sculpted effigy at prayer, his house, and a plan of the associated sites — recorded before much of old Paris was rebuilt.

## Iconography
The composite sheet shows Flamel kneeling in prayer as a carved effigy, set beside an elevation of his house and a topographical plan locating his foundations. It is the work of an eighteenth-century antiquary intent on preserving the physical traces of the legend.

## Significance
Drawings like this are how the [[motif:flamel monument|Flamel monuments]] survive at all: many of the actual stones and arches were destroyed in later centuries, and the [[motif:antiquarian record|antiquarian record]] becomes the principal witness. They show the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|legend]] being curated and mapped onto the real topography of Paris.

## For artists & game designers
A period 'collector's plate' device: effigy, building, and map on one sheet, doubling as an in-game quest log of a single figure's landmarks.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: The Charnel House of the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents
entries.creator: Theodor Josef Hubert Hoffbauer
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: after T. J. H. Hoffbauer, *Paris à travers les âges*]
entries.summary:
A reconstruction of the charnel galleries (charniers) of the Cemetery of the Holy Innocents in Paris — the arcaded burial ground on one of whose arches Nicolas Flamel was said to have painted his hieroglyphic figures.

## Iconography
The view shows the long arcaded charnel-house galleries that ringed the cemetery, their attics heaped with bones and their walls bearing painted images. It was on the fourth arch of these arcades, in 1407, that Flamel claimed to have set his enigmatic paintings.

## Significance
The [[motif:innocents cemetery|Innocents]] was the most crowded burial ground in medieval Paris, famous for its painted [[motif:danse macabre|Danse Macabre]] and its overflowing [[motif:ossuary|ossuaries]]. Siting his coded alchemical programme amid this great public theatre of death let Flamel hide the Great Work in plain sight; the cemetery was cleared in 1786 and the arch lost, surviving only through engravings.

## For artists & game designers
A macabre hub-level: a bone-filled arcaded gallery whose painted arches each conceal a puzzle behind a memento-mori surface.

citations.text: Vanessa Harding, *The Dead and the Living in Paris and London, 1500–1670* (2002).

entries.title: Portal of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie
entries.creator: after Abbé Villain
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: from Abbé Villain, *Histoire critique de Nicolas Flamel et de Pernelle sa femme* (1761)]
entries.summary:
An engraving of the portal of the church of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie, Flamel's parish church, which he helped to endow and decorate — from Abbé Villain's critical history of Flamel.

## Iconography
The plate records the gothic doorway of the now-demolished church, with carved sculpture and the kind of donor inscriptions Flamel attached to the buildings he funded. Only the great bell-tower, the Tour Saint-Jacques, still stands.

## Significance
Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie was the focus of [[motif:flamel benefaction|Flamel's benefactions]] and, by tradition, a repository of his carved emblems; Abbé Villain's 1761 *Histoire critique* used such records to mount the first sceptical, documentary account of the legend. The portal is thus both a monument of Flamel's real piety and a touchstone of the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|critical scholarship]] that grew up around him.

## For artists & game designers
A lost-building plate: a richly carved church door, since demolished, that the player explores only through an old engraving.

citations.text: Abbé Étienne Villain, *Histoire critique de Nicolas Flamel et de Pernelle sa femme* (Paris, 1761).

entries.title: Monogram of Nicolas Flamel, Tour Saint-Jacques
entries.creator: Anonymous (medieval carving); photo by Ibex73
entries.summary:
A carved monogram associated with Nicolas Flamel on the Tour Saint-Jacques, the surviving bell-tower of his parish church of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie in Paris.

## Iconography
The close photograph shows a carved cipher among the tower's late-gothic stonework, read by tradition as Flamel's mark. The Flamboyant tower (built 1509–23) post-dates Flamel himself, but stands on the church to which he was the great benefactor, and so gathers his name.

## Significance
The [[motif:tour saint-jacques|Tour Saint-Jacques]] is the most conspicuous surviving link to the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|Flamel legend]] in modern Paris: a soaring gothic landmark that fixes the alchemist in the city's topography. Carved [[motif:monogram|ciphers]] like this one feed the enduring popular hunt for Flamel's hidden signs in Parisian stone.

## For artists & game designers
A findable glyph on a real monument: a carved cipher the player photographs and decodes as part of an urban-legend trail.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).

entries.title: The Tour Saint-Jacques, Paris
entries.creator: Photograph by Benh Lieu Song
entries.summary:
The Tour Saint-Jacques, the lone Flamboyant-gothic bell-tower that survives from the church of Saint-Jacques-de-la-Boucherie, long bound up with the legend of Nicolas Flamel.

## Iconography
The slender, richly pinnacled tower (1509–23) rises alone in its square, all that remains after the church was demolished during the Revolution. Its sculpture and gargoyles have invited generations of esoteric 'readings' connecting it to Flamel's alchemy.

## Significance
As the great surviving fragment of Flamel's [[motif:pilgrim church|parish church]] — a starting point of the pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela, where the legend places Flamel's decisive encounter — the [[motif:tour saint-jacques|tower]] became the chief Parisian shrine of the [[motif:scrivener-alchemist|Flamel myth]], celebrated by nineteenth-century occult revivalists and still pointed out on every esoteric tour of the city.

## For artists & game designers
An iconic vertical landmark for an occult-Paris setting: a solitary carved tower that anchors a whole district's secret-history quests.

citations.text: Nigel Wilkins, *Nicolas Flamel: Des livres et de l'or* (1993).
